module.title: REGEX & TEXT
module.subtitle: gsub · scan · match? · split · format
module.theory:
## Patterns in the noise
Text is the wire format of the net. Ruby gives you a clean toolkit for slicing, matching and rebuilding it. A **regex** (regular expression) is a pattern written between slashes: `/cat/` matches the letters c-a-t anywhere in a string.

## Search: match? and =~
**match?** asks a yes/no question and returns **true** or **false** — fast and clean.
~~~ruby
"NEON-7".match?(/[0-9]/)   # true  (contains a digit)
"abc".match?(/[0-9]/)      # false
~~~
The older **=~** operator returns the *index* of the first match, or **nil** if there is none. Because nil is falsy, it also works in an `if`.
~~~ruby
"abc7" =~ /[0-9]/   # 3   (digit is at index 3)
"abc"  =~ /[0-9]/   # nil
~~~

> INTEL — Reach for **match?** when you only need true/false. It is faster and reads like plain English.

## Replace: gsub and sub
**gsub** replaces *every* match; **sub** replaces only the *first*. Both **return a new string** — the original is untouched.
~~~ruby
"a-b-c".gsub("-", "_")   # "a_b_c"   (all)
"a-b-c".sub("-", "_")    # "a_b-c"   (first only)
"r2d2".gsub(/[0-9]/, "#") # "r#d#"   (regex)
~~~

> WARNING — gsub does NOT change the string in place; it hands you a fresh one. `s.gsub("x","y")` alone does nothing useful — you must keep the result: `s = s.gsub("x","y")`. (The bang versions `gsub!`/`sub!` mutate, but for now prefer returning a new value.)

## Collect: scan
**scan** walks the whole string and returns an **array** of every match.
~~~ruby
"a1 b2 c3".scan(/[0-9]/)   # ["1", "2", "3"]
"id-42 id-99".scan(/[0-9]+/) # ["42", "99"]  (+ means one or more)
~~~

## Break apart & rejoin: split and join
**split** cuts a string into an array on a separator; **join** glues an array back into a string.
~~~ruby
"red,green,blue".split(",")   # ["red", "green", "blue"]
["a", "b", "c"].join("-")      # "a-b-c"
~~~

## Tidy the edges: strip
**strip** returns a copy with leading and trailing whitespace removed (it does NOT touch spaces in the middle).
~~~ruby
"  hello  ".strip   # "hello"
~~~

## Templates: format and %
Build a string from a template with **format(...)** (or its twin, the **%** operator). `%s` inserts a string, `%d` an integer, `%05d` pads an integer to width 5 with zeros.
~~~ruby
format("USER %s :: LVL %d", "Neo", 7)   # "USER Neo :: LVL 7"
"ID-%05d" % 42                          # "ID-00042"
~~~

## Quick checks: start_with? and include?
**start_with?** tests the beginning; **include?** tests anywhere inside.
~~~ruby
"NEON-CITY".start_with?("NEON")  # true
"NEON-CITY".include?("CITY")     # true
~~~

> INTEL — These predicate methods end in **?** and return true/false. That trailing `?` is just part of the name in Ruby.

exercises.title: SCRUB STATIC
exercises.brief: Replace every dash with a space.
exercises.prompt:
Define a method **scrub(text)** that returns a new string with **every** dash `-` replaced by a single space. Use **gsub**.
~~~ruby
scrub("de-tox-now")  # "de tox now"
~~~
exercises.starter:
def scrub(text)
  # TODO: gsub every dash to a space
  text
end

tests.name: returns a new string, original untouched
tests.code:
s = "a-b"
assert_equal(scrub(s), "a b")
assert_equal(s, "a-b", "gsub returns a copy; do not mutate the input")

exercises.lore: Clean the line before you jack in.

exercises.title: DIGIT SCAN
exercises.brief: True if the code contains any digit.
exercises.prompt:
Define a method **has_digit?(code)** that returns **true** if the string contains at least one digit (0-9), otherwise **false**. Use **match?** with the regex `/[0-9]/`.
~~~ruby
has_digit?("NEON7")  # true
has_digit?("NEON")   # false
~~~
exercises.starter:
def has_digit?(code)
  # TODO: use match? with /[0-9]/
  false
end
exercises.hint: code.match?(/[0-9]/) is already true or false.

exercises.title: FIRST HIT
exercises.brief: Index of the first digit, or -1.
exercises.prompt:
Define a method **first_digit_index(code)** that returns the **index** of the first digit using the **=~** operator with `/[0-9]/`. If there is no digit, `=~` gives **nil** — return **-1** in that case.
~~~ruby
first_digit_index("abc7")  # 3
first_digit_index("abc")   # -1
~~~
exercises.starter:
def first_digit_index(code)
  # TODO: use =~ , handle the nil case
  0
end
exercises.hint: (code =~ /[0-9]/) || -1  — nil is falsy, so || gives the fallback.

exercises.title: HARVEST CODES
exercises.prompt:
Define a method **harvest(text)** that returns an **array of every number** found in the string, each as a string. Use **scan** with `/[0-9]+/` (the `+` means one or more digits in a row).
~~~ruby
harvest("id-42 port-99")  # ["42", "99"]
harvest("no codes")       # []
~~~
exercises.starter:
def harvest(text)
  # TODO: scan for runs of digits
  []
end
exercises.hint: text.scan(/[0-9]+/) returns an array of matches.
exercises.lore: Strip the codes from the data stream.

exercises.title: RE-ROUTE
exercises.brief: Split on commas, rejoin with arrows.
exercises.prompt:
Define a method **reroute(path)** that **splits** the string on commas, then **joins** the pieces with ` -> ` (space, arrow, space).
~~~ruby
reroute("node1,node2,node3")  # "node1 -> node2 -> node3"
~~~
exercises.starter:
def reroute(path)
  # TODO: split on "," then join with " -> "
  path
end
exercises.lore: Lay a new route through the grid.

exercises.title: ID BADGE
exercises.prompt:
Define a method **badge(name, level)** that returns a string in the form `NAME-LVL` where the level is **zero-padded to 3 digits**. Use **format** with `%s` and `%03d`.
~~~ruby
badge("Neo", 7)    # "Neo-007"
badge("Trinity", 42) # "Trinity-042"
~~~
exercises.starter:
def badge(name, level)
  # TODO: format with %s and %03d
  "#{name}-#{level}"
end
exercises.hint: format("%s-%03d", name, level) — %03d pads with zeros to width 3.

exercises.title: TRIM THE EDGES
exercises.brief: Strip ends, then test the prefix.
exercises.prompt:
Define a method **valid_command?(line)** that first **strips** leading/trailing whitespace, then returns **true** only if the cleaned text **starts with** `"CMD:"`. Use **strip** and **start_with?**.
~~~ruby
valid_command?("  CMD: run  ")  # true
valid_command?("run CMD:")      # false
~~~
exercises.starter:
def valid_command?(line)
  # TODO: strip, then check start_with?("CMD:")
  false
end
exercises.lore: Trim the noise before you trust the order.

exercises.title: REDACT
exercises.brief: Mask every digit with a hash.
exercises.prompt:
Define a method **redact(text)** that returns a new string where **every digit** is replaced by `#`, but only **if** the text contains the substring `"SECRET"`. If `"SECRET"` is not present (use **include?**), return the text unchanged. Use **gsub** with `/[0-9]/` for the masking.
~~~ruby
redact("SECRET pin 4827")  # "SECRET pin ####"
redact("public 4827")      # "public 4827"
~~~
exercises.starter:
def redact(text)
  # TODO: if it includes "SECRET", gsub digits to #
  text
end
exercises.solution:
def redact(text)
  if text.include?("SECRET")
    text.gsub(/[0-9]/, "#")
  else
    text
  end
end
exercises.hint: if text.include?("SECRET") then text.gsub(/[0-9]/, "#") else text end
exercises.lore: What they cannot read, they cannot steal.
